So, before we sign off -- what must be our biggest threat on this plan?
The single-supplier exposure. It is our worst vulnerability. A default is unlikely to happen, but if it does, we have no fallback.
Agreed. And the ports might ease by Q3, but they could just as easily get worse, couldn't they?
Exactly. So we mitigate with a two-week buffer. It may feel expensive now, but it could save the whole launch later.
What about currency? The rate can't stay flat all year.
No, it won't. A downturn could hit our margins, so finance will hedge. That safeguard means we are not caught off guard. Doing nothing is the one thing that is bound to fail.
A risk you can name is a risk you can mitigate.
It might ease by Q3, but it could just as easily get worse.
A default is unlikely, but the impact would be severe.
Let me build in a buffer so we are not caught off guard.
Doing nothing is the one option that is bound to fail.

Good morning. Before we sign off on the new sourcing plan, let me walk you through the main risks, because some of these could derail the timeline. First, the port congestion in Asia. It might ease by Q3, but honestly it could just as easily get worse, so we cannot assume smooth sailing. Second, the single-supplier exposure on the key component. If that supplier fails, we are exposed, and right now we have no fallback, which is a vulnerability we must fix. Third, currency. The exchange rate may move against us, and with our margins, even a small swing could hurt. Now, the likelihood. Port delays are highly likely, so we should plan for them. A supplier default is unlikely, but the impact would be severe, so it is worth a contingency. And a currency shock is possible but hard to predict. My recommendation is simple: we must build in a buffer, we should diversify the supplier base, and we cannot afford to ignore the downside. A risk you can name is a risk you can manage.

Hi team, here is my follow-up after the risk review, and I want to focus on what we are actually going to do about it. The biggest threat is clearly the single-supplier exposure, which keeps me up at night. We are going to qualify a second supplier this quarter; it may take longer than we hope, but the safeguard is non-negotiable. On the port congestion, we cannot control the ports, but we can build a buffer of two weeks into every shipment, so a delay does not become a crisis. That buffer might feel expensive now, but it could save the whole launch later. On currency, I have asked finance to look at hedging, because the exchange rate may swing either way and we should not be caught off guard. To be clear, none of these moves are certain to work, but doing nothing is the one option that is bound to fail. The team that anticipates the downside is the team that sleeps at night.

Marcus opens the risk review before the team signs off on the sourcing plan. He flags three threats: port congestion, single-supplier exposure, and currency swings. He weighs the likelihood and the impact of each one. He recommends a buffer, a second supplier, and a hedge. The team agrees that a risk you can name is a risk you can manage.
